you have to understand in 1995 there

well here's what we want to do

but we're gonna randomize some of those

and sometimes they're humans and because

because from a testing point of view

it was pretty clear we had to have some
new technologies we had to have a sense
of death in the world so we had to
create a type of buffering that would
run in real time we had to create
lighting so we had to create deferred
rendering and normal vectors that would
actually allow us to light the scene we
had to create real-time compositing that
would allow the Z buffers to work
together and we had to do all those
things inside of loops that would be
repeated but also lay on top of each
other so it didn't get to even get this
sense that the world kept repeating
itself over and over again that's okay
for a neon sign that blinks but it's not
so good for a car that drives by you
don't want to see the same car go back
and forth so the idea was start with
compression so we started with the
compression we said look we can make
these beautiful scenes are really lush
and really detail with all this lighting
and they look wonderful and we've
crunched them down to where we can fit
and they look great then it's like well
let's animate them okay once you animate
them of course now you end up with
several seconds of animation you have to
even better than compression so we had
to take our compression technology and
we had to actually make it more of a
trilinear compression that would look
through time so that we could make use
of the fact that blocks were repeated
over time and this was pretty innovative
when you're talking about eight or nine
seconds of video at the time even things
like MPEG would only do a couple of
frames not even not not dozens or
hundreds of frames not even just a
couple of frames so we had look ahead
that was all the way out several hundred
frames later and once we were done with
all that we had this beautiful
environment that looked amazing and it's
like okay this looks like the 3d
rendered stuff it was better than the
stuff we had done for command & Conquer
to the date and was really great now
we're gonna put a character in it and so
the idea was let's go put some sprites
in there well they look terrible okay
but the idea originally was well just
use sprites and you know kind of will
move them around and between our keenum
and everything else like we did with our
other adventure games it clearly wasn't
going to work as a minute you put a
sprite in that environment it just
didn't look right so then we said okay
well now we have the sprites they don't
work what are we gonna do instead so
when we are making the characters of
Blade Runner we had these models and I
think people are familiar with models
and polygons well we had to represent
them in a world that was 3d so we used
voxels a voxel is a single graphical
element that is a volume instead of a
single pixel and because it's a volume
it can be wrapped around us think of
Minecraft if you took minecraft and
shrunk it down that would be voxels
everything's made of little blocks the
voxels look good because a pixel by
pixel they look very much like the
scene and the the kind of blockiness you
get from tech from voxels when you go to
render them back then Vox will unify
voxel unit they look very blocky too so
they matched the kind of compression in
the scene so they felt really good what
was also nice about voxels is as we were
going around the outside of the
character at any given time we have the
normal vector of what that particular
pixel was and so we were able to
actually add another layer of
compression so we didn't just have the
scene we also had the z-buffer then on
top of that we added another layer which
was a lighting buffer sort of like a
deferred renderer that would have
lighting information so that the the
character as we're rendering the voxels
we could actually allow light to land on
the character and that kind of solved
the deal at that point when the
character like McCoy walks into the
scene and picks up all the light from
the neon and if there's a big spotlight
that's hitting the water and it's
splashing up and with all this global
illumination the character that walked
on the scene would pick up that light
and it looked very believable and so we
thought okay that's really great we're
done this is going to be amazing as we
started to build the game we started to
all the sequences we realized that there
was another thing that was missing Blade
Runner has all this atmosphere in him it
has all these clouds and steam and so
the problem with Z buffers is they're
they're z buffers they're they're well
paid right so the character would be
walking into the distance and they
wouldn't get they wouldn't be affected
by the steam they would just be stamped
out over the top of the steam so if they
walk through the cloud in the distance
they would look wrong so then we had to
add on top of all of that another system
that managed volume metrics and so we've
put Impostors basically up probes inside
the environment that were invisible when
we rendered them but that in that
information in real time was used to add
effects onto the voxel characters to
make them appear to be going into the
steam to gray them out if they were in
disty more make them go a little darker
if they went into areas that were really
heavy shadows and once we did all of
that we actually even have shadows that
could fall across the surface of an
object we got to the point where the
characters looked so believable that
they could do just about anything and so
we were finally there
it wasn't lost on the game development

the idea that when you're playing the
